item.title: Re: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded: I left the url in the file info

item.title: Re: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
That's okay, but if you could supply the URLs for where these replacement pics come from, that'd be helpful.

edit: found 'em myself - doll is https://pixabay.com/photos/doll-toys-girl-faces-dolls-head-1523829/ , building is https://pixabay.com/photos/house-haunted-house-spooky-scary-71359/

item.title: Re: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, sorry about that. I didn't quite know the rules when I created this
Edit: I uploaded a CC0 image for the building and doll

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Removed.

item.title: Image source
item.content:encoded:
as far as I can tell, this photo was taken by dollmaker and artisan Alexandra Koukinova Tanetchka, to illustrate her work; there are several more of this figure from different angles. I strongly doubt that it's Creative Commons-compatible.

edit: also, the house comes from Brian Brown's "Vanishing Georgia" blog (note that the article history shows that the original filename mentioned Brian Brown!), where the copyright notice demands you get his written permission and link to his original site.

https://vanishingsouthgeorgia.com/2012/07/14/edwin-smith-house-1888-sycamore/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is just bad. The idea is bad, the writing is bad, the execution is bad. I see no redeeming value here; full rewrite or deletion seem like the only options.

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…which is ridiculous.

yes, it is ridiculous. I'm sorry.

Well, if it's not weird it wouldn't be an SCP, would it? About the inconsistency, like I said, the point is that the skip itself was modeled to have irrational mentality.There's no fixed base about what will or will not turned into dolls and stored or not by the object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
aren't you a bit, three years late on this?

No. Unless you're suggesting that the discussion on an article be closed after a certain amount of time, which is ridiculous.

Also, the problem isn't that the object is weird, it's that the description is internally inconsistent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
aren't you a bit, three years late on this?

The way I see it the author wanted to make the automaton less logical and going more with feelings, the way a real girl would do when choosing dolls. Girls are picky, and scary. That's the selling point that separates it from other doll-making scips.

Indeed, this is simply A that turn B into C, and it wasn't really that interesting, but you ought to remember that it's on series 2 SCP, which means something that would fail as series 3 won't necessarily fail on its era.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, so if mammals or birds enter the home, then it will drag them into the home, except when it won't? I am confus. Also, this is mostly a not-terribly-interesting mashup of various object cliches. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, even if they think it's relatively safe, surely they'd send a D-class in rather than a researcher?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i feel like this is my favorite SCP, I love the concept of it turning you into a doll.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the (us intended or intended?)word play on dollhouse you reverse the roles the doll is the person and the humans are her playthings

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Echoing this; also, the writing is very clunky in places.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First image is broken, but the rest of the SCP is good. I'm a bit tired of "waiting for people who died a long time ago" thing, but this instance is passable enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The picture of the doll feels off to me in a way that I can not pin down.
Is that a life-size doll, a real person made-up to look doll-like, or what?
I see a few stray hairs off the top of her head, and the focus seems consistent, so I'm thinking it's not photoshoppery. But it's way too life-like for the macro dolls I've seen, and one hell of an accomlishment if it's a live lady cosplaying Barbie.

Story itself is a bit cliche, but solidly executed and avoids the biggest pitfalls. No rage state or flesh-doll gore, for example. +1

Edit: Just a note that I was talking about a former picture. The current one is just a close up of a standard fashion doll.
Also, I guess that picture was a big part of what I liked. Or maybe my tastes have changed over the past six years. Re-reading today I just find it kind of boring. Swap to -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Basically this, but upvoted for potential and for the feels.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The biology terminology fails pretty hard for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one finally got posted, huh?
Liked it for a while, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you should say the doll is animate, also I would like to suggest describing whether it moves….unnaturally, or some sort of machinery. Also, if the idea of taking away the interview is accepted, it might work to replace it with some letters or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did some editing. Hopefully it has improved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm with Romanian Folk Dances here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only one other skip does the turn into dolls, which is SCP-747.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm personally not a fan. I feel like the 'turns you into a doll' idea has been done better in other skips. 1851 certainly has potential to tug on a few different fears than the ones that already exist but, until this gets made better. It's a tentative downvote.

Don't mind me. I probably don't know what I'm talking about .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Interview log is General Harlands part in this collab, so yeah

So talk to him about it (I think it's great imho)

…

What do you think discussion pages are for? :p

Also, use the edit button next time please.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You still have some SCP-XXX's in there.

I am skeptical of the presence of the fact it now communicates. I kind of liked thinking it was a kind of automaton for something that no longer exists. It would've had the same fruitless, relentless effort of a life-support machine attached to a corpse. Except it is a dollmaker who makes dolls for a child that is no longer. Giving fuel to a childhood that is no more.

You diiiig? But, hm, all of the other things I feel muddles it up. Like the interview log, etc.

I feel that in order to achieve the effect you may be looking for (if it is the same one I described earlier in my post) you may have to cut out the things that are trying to draw the reader in with. It is the simplicity of language that distances childhood book from reader, but it is also that distance that reminds the reader of a pretty fantasy childhood that they cannot exist in, that they can only imagine about, and to spark a reader's imagination is a great and lovely thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still think there's nothing interesting enough here to justify the amount of cliche present. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So talk to him about it (I think it's great imho)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Interview log is General Harlands part in this collab, so yeah

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to upvote this, because I love the imagery— but the interview log just makes the narrative too explicit. I'd prefer if it had left off with some ambiguity, and the doll being aware of what she's doing just spoils it a bit, for me. Blue and orange morality would be more interesting.
